headlines.title: Woman, 84, Finds Relatives After 54-Year Separation
headlines.subtitle: Family, Scattered by War, to Be Reunited-Aged Resident of Third Ward Great Friend of the Street Car Men.
headlines.fullText: If you had been a young person and you bade farewell to your sisters and brothers and hurriedly departed to another state in the confusion of war If communication was cut off in the county, and you lost all sight of your loved ones In the dark days of reconstruction- If you lived through half a century Ignorant of whether your sisters were alive or dead, without the slightest hope of ever finding them- And if you discovered then that they were alive and you were going to visit them after the long separation You would have a feeling of strange joy, wouldn't you? Mrs. Elizabeth Madigan, 1550 Crump street, will have lived through this experience if nothing happens before Aug. 1 to prevent her seeing her brothers living on a farm near Ardmore, Okla. Saw One 54 Years Ago. She is 84 years old when she saw one of her sisters for the first time in fifty-four years. It was then that she learned her brothers were living, but she hasn't seen them yet. Fifty-four years ago, she saw her brother George in Red river county, last. Forty-five years ago, in Ellis county, she saw her brother Marion last. Mrs. Madigan has been a widow five years. Her husband was a railroad man. Since her husband's death, Mrs. Madigan has lived alone in the little house that she has owned for thirty years. She was born in New York. She went with her parents to Hot Springs, Ark. When the war began she was brought to Texas. Her sisters and brothers all were separated. After the war, she married Dennis Madigan, who had fought through the war in a Maine regiment and who had decided to cast his lot with the South. "He came to this country direct from Ireland. He couldn't speak straight English. He spoke with an Irish brogue." About ten years ago Mrs. Madigan saw one of her sisters for the first time in fifty-four years. It was then that she learned her brothers were living, but she hasn't seen them yet. Fifty-four years ago, she saw her brother George in Red river county, last. Forty-five years ago, in Ellis county, she saw her brother Marion last. Mrs. Madigan has been a widow five years. Her husband was a railroad man. Since her husband's death, Mrs. Madigan has lived alone in the little house that she has owned for thirty years. She gets a pension. Besides this, she does washing to add to her income. Mrs. Madigan is a picturesque and quaint old character. All the Third ward knows her. Knows the Street Car Men. At her age, one might think without seeing her that she is feeble. But she is not. She is a robust old woman, with a smiling face and always ready to exchange a joke with the motorman on the Third ward line. "That fat fellow's a great tease," she said to a visitor as a car passed and she answered the salute of the motorman. 'One day I was taking a plate of victuals to a sick lady and he stopped the car and said "Give me that, grandma." Well, of all the nerve! But I gave it to him. He got some more for the man, and I give 'em hot buns and turkey and chicken and things whenever I have them. Sometimes, I give 'em blackberry pudding and you ought to see 'em eat it."

headlines.title: MASS MEETING TO ARRANGE FOR WATER CARNIVAL PROPOSED
headlines.fullText: "A mass meeting to discuss a big water carnival as a fall feature following the completion of the West Fork dam is proposed by Tillman Graham, promoter of the Waco Cotton Palace. The scene of the proposed water carnival would be Lake Minnetonka, at the West Fork dam, according to Graham's plan. The place would be so improved as to serve as a compliment to the National Feeders and Breeders' show. Graham proposes a stock company, for which a capital stock of $100,000 would be ample. The company would erect a pavilion for dancing and other things of interest. He also proposes the erection of a loop the loop and other amusement contrivances. Concessions would help to bring in revenue, and the patronage that would come to such a place during Fat Stock Show week would be amply sufficient to make it a paying investment, he thinks. The hill just north of the dam would prove an ideal location for such an enterprise. From it a view of almost the entire city of Fort Worth is afforded. "The stock show is a great attraction", he said Saturday, "and I have no thought of competing with it. The sort of thing I am advocating would merely increase the crowds that come to Fort Worth for it, and hold them here a longer time. The water carnival would be a different sort of entertainment offered and would draw many who wanted a change from the regular routine." He proposes to lease a portion of the 2,400 acres owned by the city near the dam and to get permission from the commission to use the lake for boating purposes. He has consulted a member of the board of engineers, he says, and has been assured that boating on the lake would be attended by no contamination of the water that could be noticed by consumers. In case the project is carried out, the Northern Texas Traction company expresses a willingness to extend a line to the place, and the business men are said to be in favor of the plan.

headlines.title: BOND ISSUES CARRY
headlines.subtitle: Money for Schools and Street Improvement Voted in Vernon.
headlines.fullText: VERNON, Texas, July 19.-Complete returns from the city election show that the street improvement and school house bonds carried. The school house bonds received 142 for and 45 against, while the street improvement bonds received 126 for and 60 against. The bonds are for $16,000 for each of the school building and street improvements. There are nearly 600 voters in the city but only 187 votes were cast in this election. Everything is in readiness for the initial meeting of the Wilbarger county race meet at Vernon, beginning Tuesday, July 22, and ending Aug. 1. Both the Denver and Frisco roads have granted a rate of one and one-third for the event. The racers that now are in the race meet at Childress will appear here and $1,000 in purses is offered.

headlines.title: ARMY MANEUVERS START IN VIRGINIA
headlines.subtitle: Two Months Will Be Required for Tests of Foreign Methods.
headlines.fullText: WINCHESTER, Va., July 19.-Two months of cavalry maneuvers began here tomorrow to work out the results of study by American officers of the Russian and German cavalry organizations. Troops of the Twentieth cavalry arrived today from Fort Ethan Allen, Vermont, the Eleventh cavalry from Fort Oglethorpe, Georgia, last night and two more regiments are due tomorrow. Through the broad Shenandoah valley bugles will ring and the squadrons will learn how cavalry may best be used in hostilities. In the early seventies the European formations, which served through the Civil war, were abandoned. Some of the results of the maneuvers may be to restore them, partially at least.

headlines.title: GREEN PENNANTS HELP OAKWOOD CHAPEL FUND
headlines.subtitle: Money Goes to Pay for Cemetery Building Erected for Public Use.
headlines.fullText: The fund for paying the debt on Oakwood Cemetery chapel is being augmented by the sale of green and white Oakwood pennants. The pennants are sold by The Star-Telegram for $1 each. The money realized from the sale of pennants will be applied directly to the Oakwood fund. The fund is being raised by friends of the cemetery to make a payment of $1,900 on the chapel. The chapel was erected recently at the entrance of Oakwood cemetery. It is for the use of the public. The friends of the chapel and all others who wish to contribute to a good cause will find no more worthy one than the Oakwood chapel fund. The friends of the chapel have worked hard to get the money to pay for the chapel, but they have about exhausted their resources. They need the help that will accrue to them through the sale of Oakwood pennants. All pennants ordered by mail or telephone through The Star-Telegram will be delivered by the pennant secretary.

headlines.title: WE HAVEN'T ENOUGH TO BLOW UP OUR RIVALS
headlines.subtitle: Congressman Fears Lack of Torpedoes Will Endanger Nation in Time of War.
headlines.fullText: BOSTON, July 19.-"There are not enough torpedoes in this country today to rent one round in a combat with another nation," said Congressman Fred A. Britten of Illinois, a member of the house naval committee, today at the Charlestown navy yard. With the others of the committee Mr. Britten is making an inspection of the naval stations on the Atlantic coast. "Immediately upon my return to Washington I shall introduce an order to double the capacity of the torpedo station at Newport."

headlines.title: SOCIALISTS TO HOLD STATE ENCAMPMENT
headlines.subtitle: Eugene Debs and Emil Seidel Among Speakers for Meeting.
headlines.fullText: With all the attractions of a carnival, the annual encampment of the Socialists of Texas will be held at Grand Saline, Aug. 18 to 23. In some respects the encampment will resemble an old time camp meeting, but there will be economic discussions instead of theological. Arrangements already are being made for the various amusements and many committees have been appointed to make preparations. The grounds will be about one mile from the Texas & Pacific Park station. This park, which is the place where the encampment was held last year, is well shaded and well supplied with good, pure water. There will be stores, barber shops, and there will also be numerous restaurants. Three times a day there will be speeches on some phase of Socialism. Some of the speakers announced are Eugene V. Debs, Emil Seidel, A. W. Ricker, Mrs. Kate O'Hare and George Kirkpatrick.

headlines.title: ROCKEFELLER TO PAY FOR WORLD'S HEALTH
headlines.subtitle: International Health Campaign Will Be Financed by N. Y. Foundation.
headlines.fullText: NEW YORK, July 19.-The International Health Commission referred to by Ambassador Page in his speech in England is to be conducted by the Rockefeller Foundation. This was admitted today by Starr J. Murphy, who has charge of the charities of John D. Rockefeller. The foundation, Mr. Murphy said, intended specially to make a study of public health problems throughout the world similar to that already instituted in the United States.

headlines.title: WORKMEN HAMMER ON DYNAMITE; 3 KILLED
headlines.subtitle: Twelve-Pound Sledge Used in Trying to Force Explosive Into Hole.
headlines.fullText: ELKINS, W. Va., July 19.-Dynamite on which a workman was hammering with a twelve-pound sledge trying to force it into a hole, let go yesterday, and according to information received here three men were killed.
